issues.title: Ekr feedback on -02
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
issues.body:
[Archive link](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tls/uCFORHNcQE3Bw3I0bkv2j7rpJ1A/)

> TBH, I don't find this draft that helpful. While there are some parts
> of it that seem like they might be unobjectionable, way too much of it
> seems to be concerned with litigating various disputes about specific
> past events. That's not a good starting point for reform.
> 
> More generally, this draft seems to envision some defined role in the
> WG process for "Verifiers" other than through the FATT. For example:
> 
>>    The FATT process restricts the Verifier from contacting the FATT
>>    directly.  We argue that the Verifier should be allowed to contact
>>    the FATT (at least the FATT person for a specific draft) because of
>>    the following reasons:
> 
> I don't think this is advisable. In general, the IETF convention is
> that people participate as individuals and if they are to have
> specific roles (e.g., author), those roles are provided by the
> WG process, not by the nature of the person's work. I don't think
> we need the concept of a separate "Verifier" role any more than
> we have the concept of a separate "Implementor" role. We are all
> WG members.
> 
> From my perspective, the purpose of having a separate FATT is to
> ensure that in cases where the WG deems some formal analysis is
> needed, we have a structure for getting that. It does not, however,
> empower FATT members with extra authority, and that process takes
> place within the broad overall management of the chairs.

comments.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
comments.body:
> this draft seems to envision some defined role in the
WG process for "Verifiers" other than through the FATT.

Clarified explicitly in [-03](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-usama-tls-fatt-extension-03.html#section-2.2) as follows:

> Note that it is NOT a new formal role in the WG process.

issues.title: John feedback on -02
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
issues.body:
[Archive link](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tls/IxP0WVCggttufbn2U6E1bzLu3aI/)

>  Good with a discussion on formal analysis.
> 
> >explicitly indicate on a very prominent place, preferably abstract, whether the draft has gone through FATT review or not,
> 
> I think formal analysis should be treated like any other form of analysis and referenced where appropriate, for example in the Security Considerations section.
> 
> While I think it is excellent that the IETF is using formal analysis as a tool, I am seeing a tendency toward placing too much faith in it. Like all security proofs, formal analysis is only as strong as its assumptions and model. The scope is typically limited, and the model does not necessarily capture real-world deployment complexity, implementation details, operational constraints, or misuse scenarios.
> 
> The public formal verification effort around RFC 8446 was highly valuable and resulted in a number of publicly accessible papers and improved understanding of the protocol. However, my impression is that the experience with the current FATT process has been more mixed.

issues.title: Expand informal security goals
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
issues.body:
[Archive link](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tls/EJac2n1cyZgaeTQaSN4DXENAZFg/)

> Since we are not very familiar with security-related technologies, would you like to help us improve the content of security considerations?

issues.title: Ekr review of -05
issues.author: muhammad-usama-sardar
issues.body:
[Archive link](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tls/lVD0Z1fss-zsuFHuLkM6lV4pG_E/)

> On Wed, Apr 22, 2026 at 3:59 PM Muhammad Usama Sardar <
> muhammad_usama.sardar@tu-dresden.de> wrote:
> 
> > I don't see your points captured precisely in the 125 meeting notes [2].
> > So I would like to better understand your main concern. The notes capture
> > you "resisted" creating a formal "Verifier" role which I clarified
> > explicitly in both draft [3] as well as presentation [4].
> >
> Disclaiming it isn't the same as clarifying it. Let's start with S 1.
> 
>    Specifically, the TLS FATT process does not outline the division of
>    responsibility between the authors and the team doing the formal
>    analysis (the latter is hereafter referred to as the "Verifier").
>    This document aims to propose some solutions without putting an
>    extensive burden on either party.
> 
> There just isn't any such division of responsibility, because the Verifier
> doesn't exist as a concept in the WG the way that authors or editors
> do. See also Section 7. The Verifier doesn't have *any* responsibilities,
> any more than any other WG member does.
> 
> As another example, see Section 3.6.
> 
>    So at least some time should be allocated in the meetings for
>    discussion of formal analysis.
> 
>    If the authors are doing the formal analysis themselves, it would be
>    helpful to also present the current state of formal analysis in
>    meetings for discussion.  This may be a single slide describing:
> 
>    *  Approach used: symbolic or computational
> 
>    *  Tool used: ProVerif, CryptoVerif etc.
> 
>    *  Properties established
> 
>    This will help the Verifier give any feedback and avoid any
>    repititive effort.
> 
> If there is some formal analysis result to present, I would expect
> and hope that the chairs would make time for it, just as they would
> make time for an implementation report. I do not think that a
> document calling that out specially is good.
> 
> Basically everywhere in this document that it says the Verifier can
> or should do something needs to go, as it's either redundant
> (because any WG member can do it) or creates a special role
> for the Verifier.
> 
> 
> When I am saying that I (and in general Verifier) should be able to contact
> > FATT, I am not at all asking for an *exclusive* access to FATT. I just
> > don't think many members of the WG would like to hear discussions about
> > injective correspondences or correlation properties. But anyone interested
> > should be welcome. I believe the FATT-er the WG gets, the lesser disputes
> > we will have, the more productively we will work and the better the quality
> > of our drafts will be. I think we are probably in agreement, no?
> > How about having a separate public list where the FATT and interested WG
> > members interact publicly? The discussions will be publicly archived for
> > anyone interested in just checking but not subscribing to that list.
> >
> 
> As I said in the meeting, I think there are reasonable arguments to be
> made about how the FATT ought to engage with the WG, and ceteris
> paribus, I think it's better for things to be open.
> 
> However, generally when we have closed design teams we don't insist
> that they have a list where the rest of the WG can talk to them; that's
> what
> it means for the design team to be closed! So, it seems to me that what
> you're actually asking is for FATT to be an open design team. It seems
> to me that RFC 2418 places this within the chair's discretion.
> 
> Please correct me if I have misunderstood something you said. And if your
> > concern on contacting FATT is something else, please clarify.
> >
> Well, this isn't my only concern with this draft. For example, the majority
> of S 3 is relitigating various events in this and other WGs. I don't think
> that's helpful at all. As I've said earlier, I think much of the text in S 5
> is far too directive. It might or might not be a good idea for people to
> include this stuff in their drafts, but that doesn't mean that we should
> require people to do it.
> 
> -Ekr
